stem: Consider the poset of all subsets of a set with \( [[n]] \) elements, ordered by inclusion \( (\subseteq) \), whose Hasse diagram is the \( [[n]] \)-dimensional cube. How many COMPARABLE pairs \( \{S,T\} \) of distinct subsets are there in this poset (that is, pairs with \( S \subsetneq T \) or \( T \subsetneq S \))?
solution: For each of the \( [[n]] \) elements, a pair \( (S,T) \) with \( S \subseteq T \) puts it in exactly one of 3 states (in neither, in \(T\) only, or in both), giving \( 3^{[[n]]} \) pairs with \( S \subseteq T \) (including \( S=T \)). Removing the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) pairs where \( S=T \) leaves \( 3^{[[n]]} - 2^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \) STRICT containments, and each comparable UNORDERED pair corresponds to exactly one such strict containment.
distractor_rationales: This counts EVERY pair of distinct subsets, comparable or not.
This is just the number of elements (subsets) in the poset, not the number of comparable pairs.
This counts ordered pairs \( (S,T) \) with \( S \subseteq T \) but forgets to remove the \( S=T \) diagonal.
This is the number of INCOMPARABLE pairs, the complement of what was asked.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of \( [[n]] \) to the correct count.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment of \( [[n]] \) from the correct count.

stem: Consider the poset of all subsets of a set with \( [[n]] \) elements, ordered by inclusion \( (\subseteq) \). How many INCOMPARABLE pairs \( \{S,T\} \) of distinct subsets are there (pairs with neither \( S \subseteq T \) nor \( T \subseteq S \))?
solution: There are \( \binom{2^{[[n]]}}{2} \) pairs of distinct subsets in total, and \( 3^{[[n]]} - 2^{[[n]]} \) of them are COMPARABLE (one contains the other). The incomparable pairs are the rest: \( \binom{2^{[[n]]}}{2} - (3^{[[n]]} - 2^{[[n]]}) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This IS the number of comparable pairs, the complement of what was asked.
This counts EVERY pair of distinct subsets, comparable or not, without removing the comparable ones.
This is just the number of elements (subsets) in the poset, not a pair count.
This is the count from the \( 3^n \) containment identity BEFORE subtracting the diagonal, and is not a pair count at all.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of \( [[n]] \) to the correct count.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment of \( [[n]] \) from the correct count.

stem: Consider the poset of all subsets of a set with \( [[n]] \) elements, ordered by inclusion, and its Hasse diagram (an edge joins \( S \) to \( T \) exactly when \( T \) covers \( S \), i.e. \( S \subsetneq T \) with \( |T|=|S|+1 \)). How many edges does this Hasse diagram have?
solution: Each of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) subsets \( S \) is covered by exactly \( [[n]]-|S| \) larger subsets (one for each element you could add). Summing \( [[n]]-|S| \) over all subsets \( S\) (equivalently, counting from the other end) gives \( [[n]]\cdot 2^{[[n]]-1} = [[answer]] \) covering edges.
distractor_rationales: This is just the number of elements (subsets) in the poset, not the number of Hasse-diagram edges.
This is the number of ALL comparable pairs (not just covering / adjacent pairs) in the poset.
This counts every pair of distinct subsets, comparable or not.
This uses \( 2^{[[n]]} \) instead of \( 2^{[[n]]-1} \), doubling the true edge count.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of \( [[n]] \) to the correct count.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment of \( [[n]] \) from the correct count.

stem: Consider the poset of all subsets of a set with \( [[n]] \) elements, ordered by inclusion. A MAXIMAL chain is a sequence \( \varnothing = S_0 \subsetneq S_1 \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq S_{[[n]]} = \{1,\ldots,[[n]]\} \) where each \( S_i \) covers \( S_{i-1} \) (one element is added at each step) all the way from the bottom element to the top element of the poset. How many distinct maximal chains does this poset have?
solution: A maximal chain from \( \varnothing \) to the full set corresponds exactly to an ORDER in which the \( [[n]] \) elements are added one at a time, so the number of maximal chains equals the number of orderings of the \( [[n]] \) elements: \( [[n]]! = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just the number of elements (subsets) in the poset, not the number of maximal chains through it.
This is the number of Hasse-diagram EDGES, not the number of full top-to-bottom chains.
This counts every function from the elements added to \( [[n]] \) steps, over-counting because it allows repeats.
This omits the very last element that must be added to reach the full set.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of \( [[n]] \) to the correct count.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment of \( [[n]] \) from the correct count.

stem: Let \( N = 2^{[[a]]}\cdot 3^{[[b]]} = [[N]] \), and consider the poset of all positive divisors of \( N \), ordered by divisibility. In the Hasse diagram of this poset (an edge joins divisor \(d\) to divisor \(e\) exactly when \(e\) is \(d\) times a single prime), how many edges are there?
solution: Every divisor of \(N\) is \( 2^i 3^j \) with \( 0\le i\le [[a]], 0\le j\le [[b]] \), so the poset is a \( ([[a]]+1)\times([[b]]+1) \) grid. Each point \((i,j)\) with \(i<[[a]]\) has a covering edge to \((i+1,j)\) (multiply by 2), giving \( [[a]]([[b]]+1) \) such edges, and each point with \(j<[[b]]\) has a covering edge to \((i,j+1)\) (multiply by 3), giving \( [[b]]([[a]]+1) \) more: total \( [[a]]([[b]]+1)+[[b]]([[a]]+1) = 2\cdot[[a]]\cdot[[b]]+[[a]]+[[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just the number of DIVISORS of \(N\) (elements of the poset), not the number of Hasse-diagram edges.
This multiplies the two exponents directly, which is not the edge-count formula.
This is the number of ALL comparable-or-not pairs of divisors, not just the covering (adjacent) pairs.
This only adds the two exponents and ignores the cross term entirely.
This overcounts the edges by one.
This undercounts the edges by one.

stem: Let \( N = 2^{[[a]]}\cdot 3^{[[b]]} = [[N]] \), and consider the poset of all positive divisors of \( N \), ordered by divisibility. How many COMPARABLE pairs \( \{d,e\} \) of distinct divisors of \( N \) are there (that is, pairs with \( d \mid e \) or \( e \mid d \))?
solution: Writing each divisor as \( 2^i3^j \), a WEAK containment \( 2^{i_1}3^{j_1} \mid 2^{i_2}3^{j_2} \) (allowing equality) needs \( i_1\le i_2 \) and \( j_1\le j_2\) independently. There are \( \binom{[[a]]+2}{2} \) pairs \( i_1\le i_2 \) in \( \{0,\ldots,[[a]]\} \) and \( \binom{[[b]]+2}{2} \) pairs \( j_1\le j_2 \) in \( \{0,\ldots,[[b]]\} \), so there are \( \binom{[[a]]+2}{2}\binom{[[b]]+2}{2} \) weak-containment ordered pairs; removing the \( ([[a]]+1)([[b]]+1) \) pairs where \(d=e\) leaves \( [[answer]] \) comparable unordered pairs of distinct divisors.
distractor_rationales: This counts EVERY pair of distinct divisors, comparable or not.
This is just the number of divisors (elements of the poset), not a pair count.
This is the number of Hasse-diagram EDGES (covering pairs only), not all comparable pairs.
This multiplies the two per-coordinate binomial counts without removing the \(d=e\) diagonal, over-counting.
This overcounts the comparable pairs by one.
This undercounts the comparable pairs by one.
